title: Obstructive Sleep Apnoea
oneLiner: Repetitive pharyngeal collapse during sleep → intermittent hypoxia + sleep fragmentation; most common in overweight middle-aged men; major CV risk.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Acute hypercapnic respiratory failure (type 2 RF):** drowsy, confused, morning headache that persists; the single finding is **awake pCO₂ > 6.5 kPa** (especially if SpO₂ < 94% on room air) — do **not** give high-flow O₂ alone (will suppress hypoxic drive)  
- **Nocturnal bradyarrhythmia / high-degree AV block:** bradycardia with pauses > 3 sec seen on telemetry or ECG during apnoeas; the single finding is **heart rate < 30 bpm during sleep** with syncope or near-syncope — check ECG for heart block and consider urgent cardiology review
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Loud snoring (witnessed) – “wife kicked me out of bed”
  - Witnessed apnoeas or gasping/choking during sleep
  - Unrefreshing sleep / excessive daytime sleepiness (Epworth Sleepiness Scale ≥ 11)
  - Morning headache (due to nocturnal hypercapnia)
  - Nocturia (2–4 times/night), dry mouth on waking
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Obesity (BMI > 30), large neck circumference (men > 43 cm, women > 38 cm)
  - Crowded oropharynx (Mallampati class III–IV), retrognathia/micrognathia
  - Hypertension (often resistant)
  - Signs of right heart strain late: raised JVP, ankle oedema, parasternal heave
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Simple snoring** – no apnoeas, no daytime sleepiness, normal overnight oximetry
- **Central sleep apnoea (CSA)** – no respiratory effort during apnoeas; common in heart failure, opiate use, or stroke; **splitter:** CSA has a **Cheyne-Stokes pattern** on polysomnography and normal upper airway
- **Obesity hypoventilation syndrome (OHS)** – daytime hypercapnia (pCO₂ > 6.0 kPa) + hypoxia awake; **splitter:** awake pCO₂ > 6.0 kPa + low SpO₂ on room air → OHS, not pure OSA (though often overlap)  
- **Narcolepsy** – sudden sleep attacks + cataplexy; no snoring, no apnoeas
- **Chronic fatigue / depression** – Epworth score < 10, normal sleep study
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**  
  - Epworth Sleepiness Scale (score > 10 → high probability)  
  - STOP-BANG questionnaire (≥ 3 high risk, ≥ 5 very high risk)  
  - Check **awake SpO₂** on room air – if < 94%, do **arterial blood gas** to exclude hypercapnia
- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC (polycythaemia → chronic hypoxia)  
  - U&E, TFT (hypothyroidism mimics OSA)  
  - HbA1c (OSA linked to insulin resistance)  
  - BNP if right heart strain suspected
- **Imaging:** none required for diagnosis; lateral soft-tissue neck X-ray only if suspect retrognathia/adenoids (rarely needed)
- **Special tests:**  
  - **Overnight sleep study (polysomnography or home respiratory polygraphy)** – gold standard  
  - **Apnoea-Hypopnoea Index (AHI):**  
    Mild: 5–15 events/h  
    Moderate: 15–30 events/h  
    Severe: ≥ 30 events/h  
  - Diagnosis requires AHI ≥ 5 + symptoms OR AHI ≥ 15 (even asymptomatic)
- **Never-miss test:** **Arterial blood gas** in any obese patient with daytime somnolence, SpO₂ < 94%, or morning headache — to rule out hypercapnic respiratory failure
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - If **type 2 RF** (pCO₂ > 6.5, drowsy): start **BiPAP** (not CPAP – need pressure support) with target SpO₂ 88–92%; call respiratory or ICU  
  - If **bradyarrhythmia** with pauses: cardiac monitoring, avoid negative chronotropes; discuss cardiology for possible pacing
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**  
  - Initiate **CPAP** for moderate-to-severe OSA (AHI ≥ 15) or for mild OSA with significant symptoms  
  - Refer to sleep service for titration (auto-CPAP or in-lab titration)  
  - Check CPAP compliance – aim > 4 h/night  
  - Address contributing factors: **weight loss** (refer dietary), **alcohol avoidance** before bed, **positional therapy** (avoids supine sleep)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - CPAP is mainstay (improves symptoms, BP, CV outcomes)  
  - Mandibular advancement devices (if mild–moderate and fails CPAP)  
  - ENT referral for tonsillectomy or uvulopalatopharyngoplasty (only if obstructive anatomy)  
  - Treat comorbidities: hypertension, diabetes, obesity
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - **Fluids:** No restriction unless heart failure – 1.5 L/day if oedematous  
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40 mg SC once daily (if BMI ≤ 40; consider weight-based dosing if > 40)  
  - **Diet:** No specific restrictions – avoid heavy meals 2 h before sleep  
  - **Observations:** NEWS2 q4h; overnight **SpO₂ monitoring** on current O₂ therapy (if any)
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **No drug therapy for OSA itself** – CPAP is first-line treatment  
- **Modafinil** (200 mg PO once daily in morning) – only for **residual daytime sleepiness** despite optimal CPAP use; not routine; must be prescribed by a sleep specialist  
- **Common alternatives (for comorbidities):**  
  - Weight loss: orlistat 120 mg TID with meals (refer to bariatric services)  
  - Hypertension: avoid beta-blockers as they may worsen bradycardia; ACE-i or ARB preferred  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  - **Benzodiazepines, Z-drugs, and opioids** – all worsen OSA by reducing pharyngeal tone and suppressing respiratory drive → avoid unless unavoidable (use lowest dose, monitor SpO₂ overnight)  
  - Alcohol – same effect; advise no alcohol 3 h before bedtime  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** No specific dose changes for OSA drugs (modafinil – caution in severe hepatic impairment – reduce dose)  
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving benzodiazepines as a hypnotic (e.g., zopiclone) for "poor sleep" in undiagnosed OSA** – can precipitate acute hypercapnic respiratory arrest – always screen for OSA before prescribing
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  - Acute-on-chronic hypercapnic respiratory failure (especially during acute illness or after sedation)  
  - Nocturnal arrhythmias (bradycardia, AF)  
  - Myocardial ischaemia / stroke (due to hypoxia + BP surges)  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Systemic hypertension (resistant)  
  - Pulmonary hypertension → right heart failure (cor pulmonale)  
  - Type 2 diabetes (increased insulin resistance)  
  - Cognitive impairment, depression, motor vehicle accidents (due to sleepiness)
sections.Prognosis:
- Untreated severe OSA (AHI > 30): increased all-cause mortality, especially CV death  
- With CPAP: symptom relief within days; BP drops ~2–3 mmHg, CV risk reduced  
- Weight loss of 10% can reduce AHI by 30–50%; can be “cured” with significant weight loss or bariatric surgery
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Do screen for OSA in resistant hypertension** – treat OSA to improve BP control  
- **DO use the Epworth Sleepiness Scale** at bedside – if > 10, refer for sleep study even if slim  
- **DO ensure CPAP mask fit and humidification** – the commonest reason for non-compliance is mask discomfort; involve specialist sleep nurse
sections.Pitfalls (errors to avoid):
- **Don’t diagnose OSA on history alone** – false positives from snoring; always require objective AHI  
- **Don’t discharge a patient with new oxygen therapy without checking an ABG** – O₂ can worsen hypercapnia in unrecognised OHS/OSA  
- **Don’t prescribe sedatives or hypnotics to a patient with possible OSA** – ask about snoring/sleepiness first
sections.Safety Netting (If patient goes home):
- **Return if:**  
  - Worsening shortness of breath or chest pain  
  - New confusion, morning headaches not relieved by CPAP  
  - Any episode of syncope or near-syncope  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Sleep clinic appointment within 4 weeks (sooner if severe)  
  - If CPAP started: CPAP nurse review within 1–2 weeks for compliance check and mask fitting
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / ICU if:**  
  - NEWS2 ≥ 5 (despite CPAP)  
  - Acute confusion with respiratory acidosis (pH < 7.25)  
  - SpO₂ < 85% on optimal CPAP + 2 L O₂  
  - Signs of right heart failure (raised JVP, pedal oedema) – urgent echo and respiratory review  
- **Call crash team if:**  
  - GCS < 8  
  - Respiratory arrest / pulseless bradycardia

rawMarkdown:
# Obstructive Sleep Apnoea

**One-liner:** Repetitive pharyngeal collapse during sleep → intermittent hypoxia + sleep fragmentation; most common in overweight middle-aged men; major CV risk.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Acute hypercapnic respiratory failure (type 2 RF):** drowsy, confused, morning headache that persists; the single finding is **awake pCO₂ > 6.5 kPa** (especially if SpO₂ < 94% on room air) — do **not** give high-flow O₂ alone (will suppress hypoxic drive)  
- **Nocturnal bradyarrhythmia / high-degree AV block:** bradycardia with pauses > 3 sec seen on telemetry or ECG during apnoeas; the single finding is **heart rate < 30 bpm during sleep** with syncope or near-syncope — check ECG for heart block and consider urgent cardiology review

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Loud snoring (witnessed) – “wife kicked me out of bed”
  - Witnessed apnoeas or gasping/choking during sleep
  - Unrefreshing sleep / excessive daytime sleepiness (Epworth Sleepiness Scale ≥ 11)
  - Morning headache (due to nocturnal hypercapnia)
  - Nocturia (2–4 times/night), dry mouth on waking
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Obesity (BMI > 30), large neck circumference (men > 43 cm, women > 38 cm)
  - Crowded oropharynx (Mallampati class III–IV), retrognathia/micrognathia
  - Hypertension (often resistant)
  - Signs of right heart strain late: raised JVP, ankle oedema, parasternal heave

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Simple snoring** – no apnoeas, no daytime sleepiness, normal overnight oximetry
- **Central sleep apnoea (CSA)** – no respiratory effort during apnoeas; common in heart failure, opiate use, or stroke; **splitter:** CSA has a **Cheyne-Stokes pattern** on polysomnography and normal upper airway
- **Obesity hypoventilation syndrome (OHS)** – daytime hypercapnia (pCO₂ > 6.0 kPa) + hypoxia awake; **splitter:** awake pCO₂ > 6.0 kPa + low SpO₂ on room air → OHS, not pure OSA (though often overlap)  
- **Narcolepsy** – sudden sleep attacks + cataplexy; no snoring, no apnoeas
- **Chronic fatigue / depression** – Epworth score < 10, normal sleep study

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**  
  - Epworth Sleepiness Scale (score > 10 → high probability)  
  - STOP-BANG questionnaire (≥ 3 high risk, ≥ 5 very high risk)  
  - Check **awake SpO₂** on room air – if < 94%, do **arterial blood gas** to exclude hypercapnia
- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC (polycythaemia → chronic hypoxia)  
  - U&E, TFT (hypothyroidism mimics OSA)  
  - HbA1c (OSA linked to insulin resistance)  
  - BNP if right heart strain suspected
- **Imaging:** none required for diagnosis; lateral soft-tissue neck X-ray only if suspect retrognathia/adenoids (rarely needed)
- **Special tests:**  
  - **Overnight sleep study (polysomnography or home respiratory polygraphy)** – gold standard  
  - **Apnoea-Hypopnoea Index (AHI):**  
    Mild: 5–15 events/h  
    Moderate: 15–30 events/h  
    Severe: ≥ 30 events/h  
  - Diagnosis requires AHI ≥ 5 + symptoms OR AHI ≥ 15 (even asymptomatic)
- **Never-miss test:** **Arterial blood gas** in any obese patient with daytime somnolence, SpO₂ < 94%, or morning headache — to rule out hypercapnic respiratory failure

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - If **type 2 RF** (pCO₂ > 6.5, drowsy): start **BiPAP** (not CPAP – need pressure support) with target SpO₂ 88–92%; call respiratory or ICU  
  - If **bradyarrhythmia** with pauses: cardiac monitoring, avoid negative chronotropes; discuss cardiology for possible pacing
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**  
  - Initiate **CPAP** for moderate-to-severe OSA (AHI ≥ 15) or for mild OSA with significant symptoms  
  - Refer to sleep service for titration (auto-CPAP or in-lab titration)  
  - Check CPAP compliance – aim > 4 h/night  
  - Address contributing factors: **weight loss** (refer dietary), **alcohol avoidance** before bed, **positional therapy** (avoids supine sleep)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - CPAP is mainstay (improves symptoms, BP, CV outcomes)  
  - Mandibular advancement devices (if mild–moderate and fails CPAP)  
  - ENT referral for tonsillectomy or uvulopalatopharyngoplasty (only if obstructive anatomy)  
  - Treat comorbidities: hypertension, diabetes, obesity
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - **Fluids:** No restriction unless heart failure – 1.5 L/day if oedematous  
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40 mg SC once daily (if BMI ≤ 40; consider weight-based dosing if > 40)  
  - **Diet:** No specific restrictions – avoid heavy meals 2 h before sleep  
  - **Observations:** NEWS2 q4h; overnight **SpO₂ monitoring** on current O₂ therapy (if any)

### Drugs & Doses
- **No drug therapy for OSA itself** – CPAP is first-line treatment  
- **Modafinil** (200 mg PO once daily in morning) – only for **residual daytime sleepiness** despite optimal CPAP use; not routine; must be prescribed by a sleep specialist  
- **Common alternatives (for comorbidities):**  
  - Weight loss: orlistat 120 mg TID with meals (refer to bariatric services)  
  - Hypertension: avoid beta-blockers as they may worsen bradycardia; ACE-i or ARB preferred  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  - **Benzodiazepines, Z-drugs, and opioids** – all worsen OSA by reducing pharyngeal tone and suppressing respiratory drive → avoid unless unavoidable (use lowest dose, monitor SpO₂ overnight)  
  - Alcohol – same effect; advise no alcohol 3 h before bedtime  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** No specific dose changes for OSA drugs (modafinil – caution in severe hepatic impairment – reduce dose)  
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving benzodiazepines as a hypnotic (e.g., zopiclone) for "poor sleep" in undiagnosed OSA** – can precipitate acute hypercapnic respiratory arrest – always screen for OSA before prescribing

### Complications
- **Acute:**  
  - Acute-on-chronic hypercapnic respiratory failure (especially during acute illness or after sedation)  
  - Nocturnal arrhythmias (bradycardia, AF)  
  - Myocardial ischaemia / stroke (due to hypoxia + BP surges)  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Systemic hypertension (resistant)  
  - Pulmonary hypertension → right heart failure (cor pulmonale)  
  - Type 2 diabetes (increased insulin resistance)  
  - Cognitive impairment, depression, motor vehicle accidents (due to sleepiness)

### Prognosis
- Untreated severe OSA (AHI > 30): increased all-cause mortality, especially CV death  
- With CPAP: symptom relief within days; BP drops ~2–3 mmHg, CV risk reduced  
- Weight loss of 10% can reduce AHI by 30–50%; can be “cured” with significant weight loss or bariatric surgery

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Do screen for OSA in resistant hypertension** – treat OSA to improve BP control  
- **DO use the Epworth Sleepiness Scale** at bedside – if > 10, refer for sleep study even if slim  
- **DO ensure CPAP mask fit and humidification** – the commonest reason for non-compliance is mask discomfort; involve specialist sleep nurse

### Pitfalls (errors to avoid)
- **Don’t diagnose OSA on history alone** – false positives from snoring; always require objective AHI  
- **Don’t discharge a patient with new oxygen therapy without checking an ABG** – O₂ can worsen hypercapnia in unrecognised OHS/OSA  
- **Don’t prescribe sedatives or hypnotics to a patient with possible OSA** – ask about snoring/sleepiness first

### Safety Netting (If patient goes home)
- **Return if:**  
  - Worsening shortness of breath or chest pain  
  - New confusion, morning headaches not relieved by CPAP  
  - Any episode of syncope or near-syncope  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Sleep clinic appointment within 4 weeks (sooner if severe)  
  - If CPAP started: CPAP nurse review within 1–2 weeks for compliance check and mask fitting

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / ICU if:**  
  - NEWS2 ≥ 5 (despite CPAP)  
  - Acute confusion with respiratory acidosis (pH < 7.25)  
  - SpO₂ < 85% on optimal CPAP + 2 L O₂  
  - Signs of right heart failure (raised JVP, pedal oedema) – urgent echo and respiratory review  
- **Call crash team if:**  
  - GCS < 8  
  - Respiratory arrest / pulseless bradycardia
